widgetData.source_sentence:
3 weeks ago, experienced gradual onset of RIGHT knee pain following increased running intensity during marathon training. Pain localized to the anterior aspect of the knee, particularly beneath the patella. 

Noticed pain exacerbates with deep squatting, stair climbing, and when getting up from sitting (increased quad activity). Pain rated 6/10 with activities, 3/10 at rest. 

Activities that ease discomfort include leg elevation and ice application. 

Positive for tenderness upon palpation of the infrapatellar fat pad; crepitus noted during full knee extension. No locking or giving way of the joint. 

Range of motion: Flexion within normal limits, extension slightly limited due to discomfort. 

Patient advises sleeping on the right side aggravates symptoms, thus has been rolling onto the left. Daily activities not severely impacted, but running is now avoided. 

NO significant swelling observed, although slight warmth felt around the joint. 

SHX: 
Age: 34 
Athletic background: Consistent runner, training for a half-marathon. 

Current management includes: 
- Rest from running 
- Ice application post activities 
- Considering physiotherapy for rehabilitation and strengthening exercises. 

NEXT follow-up scheduled in 1 week to reassess symptoms and introduce functional exercises.
sentences:
Condition name: Hoffa's Syndrome
Body region: Knee
Region segments: {""}
Region sides: {Left,Right,Bilateral}
Description: Hoffa's Syndrome, also known as Fat Pad Syndrome, is a condition characterized by pain and inflammation in the infrapatellar fat pad, which is located under the kneecap (patella). This syndrome typically arises from overuse, injury, or direct trauma to the knee, often affecting athletes involved in sports that require repetitive knee movement such as running, cycling, and jumping.
Symptoms: Anterior knee pain, Swelling beneath the kneecap, Tenderness in the area of the fat pad, Pain during activities that involve knee bending or straightening, Instability in the knee, Difficulty with stair climbing or squatting
Medical abbreviations: HFS, IRF (Infrapatellar Fat Pad), PFPS (Patellofemoral Pain Syndrome), RICE (Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation)
Identifying features: Local tenderness and swelling over the infrapatellar region, exacerbated by knee flexion and extension, often with pain on palpation of the fat pad.

Condition name: Anterior Cervical Discectomy and Fusion (ACDF)
Body region: Cervical Spine
Region segments: {C1,C2,C3,C4,C5,C6,C7,T1}
Region sides: {Left,Right,Bilateral,Central,N/A}
Description: Anterior Cervical Discectomy and Fusion (ACDF) is a surgical procedure used to relieve pressure on the spinal cord or nerve roots in the cervical spine through the removal of a herniated or degenerative intervertebral disc. The procedure involves an approach from the front of the neck (anterior), discectomy (removal of the disc), and fusion (joining two or more vertebrae together using bone grafts or implants to provide stability and prevent further movement at the damaged segment). ACDF is commonly indicated for conditions like cervical radiculopathy, myelopathy, and disc herniation.
Symptoms: Neck pain, Arm pain (radiculopathy), Numbness or tingling in the arms or fingers, Weakness in the arms or hands, Restricted neck motion, Muscle spasms in the neck, Symptoms of myelopathy (e.g., clumsiness, balance issues, bladder/bowel dysfunction)
Medical abbreviations: ACDF (Anterior Cervical Discectomy and Fusion), C-spine (Cervical Spine), IVD (Intervertebral Disc), C5-C6 (referring to the specific cervical disc between vertebrae C5 and C6), MRI (Magnetic Resonance Imaging)
Identifying features: Identification of cervical disc herniation or degeneration through imaging studies (e.g., MRI or CT scan) and clinical findings related to nerve root compression such as motor and sensory deficits. The surgery typically results in decreased neck and arm pain post-operatively, and improved functional mobility.

Condition name: Trapezius Muscle Transfer
Body region: Shoulder
Region segments: {""}
Region sides: {Left,Right,Bilateral}
Description: Trapezius muscle transfer is a surgical procedure often performed to restore shoulder function and stabilize the shoulder girdle. This surgery can be indicated in cases such as brachial plexus injuries, shoulder dislocations, or other conditions where the trapezius muscle has been impaired. The trapezius muscle, which spans the upper back and neck, is responsible for moving, rotating, and stabilizing the scapula, as well as extending the neck. By transferring the muscle's function to augment other muscles, such as the shoulder stabilizers, patients may regain some degree of shoulder mobility and function.
Symptoms: Shoulder instability, Weakness in shoulder abduction, Limited range of motion in the shoulder, Pain in the shoulder area, Difficulty with overhead movements
Medical abbreviations: TMT (Trapezius Muscle Transfer), RROM (Resisted Range of Motion), ROM (Range of Motion), EMG (Electromyography), PT (Physiotherapy)
Identifying features: A surgical scar at the upper back, potential muscle atrophy in the trapezius area, observable weakness in shoulder elevation, and compensatory movements in the shoulder.

widgetData.source_sentence:
Patient presents with RIGHT elbow pain, ongoing for approximately 6-8 weeks. Initially started as a dull ache after repetitive overhead activities at work (construction). Gradually worsened in intensity, now describes it as sharp pain radiating from the posterior aspect of the shoulder down to the lateral forearm.

Patient reports occasional numbness and tingling in the RIGHT hand, particularly affecting the ring and little fingers; symptoms are worse after prolonged use of the arm, especially during lifting tasks. No reported weakness in grip strength but describes difficulty with tasks requiring fine motor skills (e.g., buttoning clothes).

24hr pattern:
- **Nights**: Significant pain when bending the arm during sleep, often awakening the patient; prefers to sleep with the arm extended.
- **Mornings**: Stiffness upon waking, improving with movement. Feels better after gentle stretching but relapses with activity.

Aggravating factors: 
- Repetitive overhead lifting
- Extended periods of arm elevation
- Sleep positioning (lying on the RIGHT side)

Easing factors:
- Rest
- Application of ice
- Avoiding overhead activities

DENIES any significant trauma, fever, or associated swelling in the elbow area. No prior history of elbow or shoulder conditions. 

SHX:
Age: 58  
Occupation: Construction worker  
Previous treatments: NSAIDs taken occasionally for relief; did not try physiotherapy prior to this visit.

Functional limitations documented, including decreased ability to perform work tasks and recreational activities (golfing). Patient expresses concern regarding the potential need for surgical intervention, but wishes to explore conservative management options first.
sentences:
Condition name: Decompression of the Suprascapular Nerve
Body region: Elbow
Region segments: {""}
Region sides: {Left,Right,Bilateral}
Description: Decompression of the suprascapular nerve is a surgical procedure aimed at relieving pressure on the suprascapular nerve, which can be compressed due to factors such as trauma, tumors, or repetitive overhead activities. This nerve innervates the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles, which are crucial for shoulder movements. When compressed, patients can experience pain and weakness in the shoulder, which can lead to decreased function in the upper limb.
Symptoms: Shoulder pain, Weakness in shoulder abduction and external rotation, Atrophy of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles, Referred pain to the elbow, Difficulty in overhead movements
Medical abbreviations: SSN - Suprascapular Nerve, SNR - Suprascapular Nerve Release, RTS - Return to Sports
Identifying features: Patients often present with localized shoulder pain, weakness in specific shoulder movements, and atrophy of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles upon examination.

Condition name: Arthroscopic Debridement
Body region: Shoulder
Region segments: {""}
Region sides: {Left,Right,Bilateral}
Description: Arthroscopic debridement of the shoulder is a minimally invasive surgical procedure performed to clean out damaged tissue, debris, or loose cartilage from the shoulder joint. This intervention is most commonly indicated for conditions such as shoulder bursitis, rotator cuff tears, or other degenerative shoulder diseases which result in the accumulation of debris and inflammation within the joint. The procedure is typically performed with the aid of an arthroscope, a small camera that allows the surgeon to visualize the inside of the joint on a monitor, utilizing small incisions for instrument access.
Symptoms: Shoulder pain, Limited range of motion, Swelling around the shoulder joint, Creaking or popping sounds during movement, Weakness in the shoulder, Difficulty performing daily activities involving overhead movements
Medical abbreviations: ARTHRO, ORIF (Open Reduction Internal Fixation), AC (Acromioclavicular), RCT (Rotator Cuff Tear), BMD (Bone Mineral Density), MRI (Magnetic Resonance Imaging)
Identifying features: Patients often present with a history of chronic shoulder pain and may exhibit tenderness over the anterior or lateral aspect of the shoulder. Physical examination may reveal restricted range of motion, muscle weakness, and signs of impingement. Imaging studies such as MRI may show rotator cuff tears or degeneration, while the presence of loose bodies or synovitis may be confirmed during an arthroscopic evaluation.

Condition name: Cubital Tunnel Release
Body region: Hand & Wrist
Region segments: {""}
Region sides: {Left,Right,Bilateral}
Description: Cubital tunnel release is a surgical procedure designed to alleviate pressure on the ulnar nerve at the elbow, which can become compressed or injured, leading to various symptoms in the hand and wrist. This procedure is typically performed when nonsurgical treatments have failed to improve the condition, which may arise from repetitive motions, direct trauma to the elbow, or anatomical variations. The surgery aims to relieve symptoms such as pain, tingling, and numbness, allowing for better function of the hand and wrist.
Symptoms: Pain in the inner side of the elbow, Numbness or tingling in the ring and little fingers, Weakness in grip strength, Difficulty with fine motor tasks, Sensitivity to pressure along the ulnar nerve pathway
Medical abbreviations: Ulnar Nerve (UN), Cubital Tunnel Syndrome (CTS), Electromyography (EMG), Nerve Conduction Study (NCS), Neurolysis (NL)
Identifying features: Key identifying features include the presence of elbow pain localized to the inner side, sensory changes in the ulnar nerve distribution (especially in the ring and little fingers), and weakness in hand grip or dexterity. A physical examination may reveal a positive Tinel's sign at the elbow.

widgetData.source_sentence:
2 weeks post-op from a C6-C7 microdiscectomy. Patient reports lingering NECK pain, primarily on the RIGHT side, which has been described as a dull ache, intensifying with prolonged sitting or looking down. Pain radiates down the RIGHT arm toward the elbow, with occasional tingling in the thumb and index finger, but no weakness noted.

Current mobility: Patient is experiencing moderate stiffness in the neck with reduced ROM, especially in flexion and rotation towards the RIGHT. Reports discomfort when turning head to check blind spots while driving and during sleep, often waking up when attempting to roll over.

Reported activities: Has been compliant with prescribed post-op restrictions but attempted light walking (up to 15-20 min daily). No reported issues with shoulder abduction or elevation, but hesitant due to past pain. 

Pain management: Utilizes ice and over-the-counter NSAIDs (Ibuprofen 400 mg TID) for pain control, with mild relief. 

Patient education: Informed on importance of posture and ergonomics, especially during prolonged sitting. Discussed positioning strategies during sleep to minimize discomfort. Encouraged to begin gentle cervical exercises as tolerated.

SHX:
Age: 45
Occupation: Office worker, primarily desk-based tasks. 

Notable: Previous history of cervical radiculopathy for 6 months prior to surgery, managed conservatively with PT and chiropractic. No current psychological distress or neuritic symptoms apart from prior mentioned. 

Physical exam: Cervical spine palpation reveals tenderness at C6-C7. Positive Spurling’s test on the RIGHT side. No signs of myelopathy.

NEXT appointment in 1 week for reassessment of symptoms and progression to strengthening exercises.
sentences:
Condition name: Cervical Microdiscectomy
Body region: Cervical Spine
Region segments: {C1,C2,C3,C4,C5,C6,C7,T1}
Region sides: {Left,Right,Bilateral,Central,N/A}
Description: Cervical microdiscectomy is a minimally invasive surgical procedure used to remove a herniated disc in the cervical spine (neck) that is pressing on spinal nerves or the spinal cord. This procedure is typically performed when conservative treatments such as physical therapy, medications, or injections fail to relieve symptoms of cervical radiculopathy, such as pain, numbness, or weakness in the arms. The aim of this surgery is to alleviate pressure on the affected nerve roots, improve mobility, and reduce pain.
Symptoms: Neck pain, Arm pain (radiculopathy), Numbness or tingling in the arms or fingers, Weakness in the arms or hands, Loss of reflexes in the arms, Difficulty with coordination or balance (in severe cases)
Medical abbreviations: C5-C6: Cervical vertebrae levels 5 and 6, MRI: Magnetic Resonance Imaging, CT: Computed Tomography, NCS: Nerve Conduction Study, EMG: Electromyography
Identifying features: Common identifying features of cervical microdiscectomy include the presence of confirmed cervical disc herniation via imaging studies, the patient's report of arm pain or neurologic deficits, and unsuccessful conservative treatment options.

Condition name: Neuroma Excision (Morton's neuroma)
Body region: Foot & Ankle
Region segments: {""}
Region sides: {Left,Right,Bilateral}
Description: Morton's neuroma is a painful condition affecting the ball of the foot, typically between the third and fourth toes. It occurs when the tissue around a nerve leading to the toes thickens, which can be caused by repetitive irritation, injury, or pressure from shoes that are too tight. Neuroma excision is a surgical procedure to remove the thickened nerve to relieve pain and discomfort.
Symptoms: Sharp, burning pain in the ball of the foot, Numbness or tingling in the toes, Sensation of standing on a pebble, Pain that worsens with activity or tight footwear, Relief of symptoms when removing shoes or massaging the foot
Medical abbreviations: NM, MCN (Morton's Compression Neuritis), ICD-10: G57.6 (Morton's neuropathy)
Identifying features: Pain localized between the third and fourth toes, typically exacerbated by wearing tight shoes or during activity.

Condition name: Cervical Foraminoplasty
Body region: Cervical Spine
Region segments: {C1,C2,C3,C4,C5,C6,C7,T1}
Region sides: {Left,Right,Bilateral,Central,N/A}
Description: Cervical foraminoplasty is a surgical procedure designed to relieve pressure on spinal nerves that can result from conditions such as herniated discs, bone spurs, or other forms of spinal stenosis in the cervical spine. The procedure involves widening the intervertebral foramen, which is the passageway through which spinal nerves exit the spinal column. By enlarging this canal, it alleviates nerve root compression, helping to reduce pain, weakness, and other neurological symptoms. This procedure often follows conservative treatment methods like physiotherapy or medication when they fail to provide relief.
Symptoms: Neck pain, Shoulder pain, Radiating pain down the arms, Numbness or tingling in the arms or hands, Muscle weakness in the arm or hand, Reduced range of motion in the neck
Medical abbreviations: C1, C2, C3, C4, C5, C6, C7, T1, MRI (Magnetic Resonance Imaging), CT (Computed Tomography), PLIF (Posterior Lumbar Interbody Fusion), ACDF (Anterior Cervical Discectomy and Fusion)
Identifying features: Identifying features of cervical foraminoplasty include a history of cervical radiculopathy, diagnostic imaging revealing foraminal narrowing or nerve root compression, and symptomatic relief demonstrated post-surgery through reduced pain and improved arm function.

widgetData.source_sentence:
Patient underwent open-heart surgery 6 weeks ago, experiencing ongoing THORACIC pain post-sternotomy. Pain localized to the sternal area, described as a constant and aching sensation, intensifying with deep breathing, coughing, or changing positions. Patient reports a feeling of tightness across the chest and difficulty with upper limb movements, especially shoulder flexion and reach overhead. 

Pain scale: 6/10 at rest, 8/10 with movement. No radiating pain reported to arms or back. 

Sleep disturbances noted, particularly when trying to lie supine or on the side. Patient prefers to prop up with pillows for support during sleep. 

Meds: Taking pain medication as prescribed post-operatively, with good adherence. 

Physical activity limited; patient has begun gentle walking for 10-15 minutes daily, which is tolerable but followed by soreness. 

No signs of infection at the sternal closure site, which appears clean and dry. 

Patient expresses concern about returning to normal daily activities and the fear of re-injury.

Age: 65 
Prior health: Previously active, enjoyed swimming and cycling, but limited upper body strength prior to surgery. 

Plan: Initiate gradual range of motion exercises for shoulders, focus on diaphragmatic breathing techniques to manage pain, monitor healing, and assess for the need for further orthopedic evaluation if symptoms persist. Follow-up in 2 weeks.
sentences:
Condition name: Sternal Reconstruction
Body region: Thoracic
Region segments: {C7,T1,T2,T3,T4,T5,T6,T7,T8,T9,T10,T11,T12,L1}
Region sides: {Left,Right,Bilateral,Central,N/A}
Description: Sternal reconstruction is a surgical procedure performed to repair or replace the sternum (breastbone) typically due to trauma, infection, or after surgical resection in cases such as cancer. The sternum serves as a protective structure for vital thoracic organs and is integral to the thoracic skeleton. The reconstruction process can involve the use of synthetic materials, bone grafts, or titanium plates to restore structural integrity and stability to the chest area.
Symptoms: Chest pain, Difficulty breathing, Sternal deformity or instability, Increased risk of respiratory infections, Scar tissue formation during healing
Medical abbreviations: CABG (Coronary Artery Bypass Grafting), CT (Computed Tomography) scan, MRI (Magnetic Resonance Imaging), Sternal Closure
Identifying features: Patients may show signs of sternal instability, abnormal chest wall contour, or have visible surgical scars. Diagnostic imaging such as X-rays or CT scans may be utilized to assess the sternum's integrity post-surgery.

Condition name: Sternal closure following open-heart surgery or sternotomy
Body region: Thoracic
Region segments: {C7,T1,T2,T3,T4,T5,T6,T7,T8,T9,T10,T11,T12,L1}
Region sides: {Left,Right,Bilateral,Central,N/A}
Description: Sternal closure following open-heart surgery or sternotomy involves the surgical technique used to close the sternum after it has been surgically split to gain access to the heart. Sternotomy is a common procedure in various cardiac surgeries, and the closure is done using steel wires, sutures, or bone plates. Proper closure is crucial for structural integrity during recovery and minimizes complications such as sternum instability or infection.
Symptoms: Chest pain or discomfort, Limited range of motion in the upper body, Swelling around the incision site, Difficulty in deep breathing, Post-surgical fatigue, Palpable hardware near the sternum (in some cases)
Medical abbreviations: CABG (Coronary Artery Bypass Grafting), MVR (Mitral Valve Replacement), AVR (Aortic Valve Replacement), CPB (Cardiopulmonary Bypass), ICU (Intensive Care Unit), PT (Physical Therapy), OT (Occupational Therapy)
Identifying features: A recent history of open-heart surgery, visible scar along the midline of the chest, tenderness or swelling around the incision area, presence of sternal wires or sutures if visible during examination.

Condition name: Latarjet Procedure (Coracoid Transfer)
Body region: Shoulder
Region segments: {""}
Region sides: {Left,Right,Bilateral}
Description: The Latarjet procedure, also known as coracoid transfer, is a surgical technique performed to stabilize the shoulder joint, primarily indicated for individuals with recurrent shoulder dislocations or significant glenoid bone loss. The procedure involves transferring a piece of the coracoid process, along with the attached musculotendinous tissue (typically the short head of the biceps), to the anterior aspect of the glenoid. This serves to increase the stability of the shoulder by augmenting the bony support as well as enhancing soft tissue restraints.
Symptoms: Recurrent shoulder dislocations or instability, Pain in the shoulder joint, Loss of range of motion, Difficulty with overhead activities, Weakness in the shoulder, Swelling or tenderness around the shoulder area
Medical abbreviations: RSD (Recurrent Shoulder Dislocation), GH (Glenohumeral), ROM (Range of Motion), AC (Acromioclavicular), RC (Rotator Cuff)
Identifying features: The Latarjet procedure is characterized by the transfer of the coracoid process to the anterior glenoid, which is often identified through imaging studies such as MRI or CT. Clinically, patients may present with a specific history of anterior shoulder instability or repeated dislocations, requiring surgical intervention.

widgetData.source_sentence:
Patient presents with LEFT hand pain following a fall while playing basketball 10 days ago. Impact directly on the LEFT hand during an attempt to break the fall. Immediate swelling and bruising noted over the 2nd and 3rd metacarpals. 

Reported difficulty with grip strength and hand function, particularly with activities of daily living (ADLs) such as opening jars and typing on a keyboard. Unable to make a full fist and experiences pain (VAS 7/10) with any movement involving finger flexion and extension.

Body chart:
- Localized tenderness over the 2nd and 3rd metacarpal regions
- Swelling present - moderate; no visible deformity noted
- No neural symptoms; sensation intact across the hand
- AROM: Restricted in MCP flexion (30°-40°), painful and limited in extension

SHX:
Age: 25
Occupation: Graphic designer - predominantly desk work
Hobbies: Basketball, painting 

Patient was advised on RICE (Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation) protocol initially. Incorporation of splint for support was initiated post-initial A&E visit. Currently scheduled for ORIF to stabilize the fracture. 

Concerns regarding recovery timeframe and return to sport expressed by the patient. Follow-up consultations planned post-surgery to assess for rehabilitation progress.

No previous history of fractures or orthopedic injuries.
sentences:
Condition name: Subtalar Fusion
Body region: Foot & Ankle
Region segments: {""}
Region sides: {Left,Right,Bilateral}
Description: Subtalar fusion is a surgical procedure that involves the fusion of the subtalar joint, which is located below the ankle joint. This joint plays a critical role in foot motion and stability, allowing for inversion and eversion of the foot. The fusion is usually performed to alleviate chronic pain due to conditions such as subtalar joint arthritis, post-traumatic arthritis, or other degenerative conditions. By fusing the joint, the motion at that specific site is eliminated, which can help reduce pain and improve function in other parts of the foot and ankle.
Symptoms: Chronic pain in the heel or ankle, Swelling around the subtalar joint, Stiffness in the foot, Limited range of motion in the ankle, Discomfort during activities such as walking or standing
Medical abbreviations: STJ (Subtalar Joint), ORIF (Open Reduction and Internal Fixation), NSAIDs (Non-Steroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drugs), PT (Physical Therapy), ROM (Range of Motion)
Identifying features: Patients often exhibit significant limitations in foot mobility, especially in inversion and eversion movements. There may also be tenderness, swelling, and a history of previous foot or ankle injuries or surgeries.

Condition name: Metacarpal Fracture ORIF
Body region: Hand & Wrist
Region segments: {""}
Region sides: {Left,Right,Bilateral}
Description: A metacarpal fracture ORIF (Open Reduction and Internal Fixation) is a surgical procedure to repair a fracture in one of the metacarpal bones, which are the long bones in the hand located between the wrist and the fingers. This type of injury often occurs due to trauma, such as falls, sports injuries, or punching an object. ORIF is typically performed to realign the fractured bone fragments accurately and stabilize them with plates and screws, allowing for proper healing and restoration of function in the hand.
Symptoms: Pain in the hand or wrist, Swelling and bruising, Deformity of the hand (e.g., angulated or rotated finger), Decreased range of motion, Difficulty in gripping or pinching, Numbness or tingling (if nerves are affected)
Medical abbreviations: ORIF - Open Reduction and Internal Fixation, RICE - Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation, ROM - Range of Motion, CT - Computed Tomography (if imaging is needed), X-ray - Radiographic imaging to assess the fracture
Identifying features: Common identifying features of a metacarpal fracture include localized swelling and tenderness over the affected metacarpal, a visible deformity such as a dorsal angulation of the finger, and limited finger mobility. X-rays are crucial in diagnosing the specific type and severity of the fracture.

Condition name: Hip Joint Lavage and Debridement
Body region: Hip
Region segments: {""}
Region sides: {Left,Right,Bilateral}
Description: Hip joint lavage and debridement is a surgical procedure that involves the cleaning of the hip joint by flushing it with a sterile fluid and removing any debris, such as damaged cartilage, floating bone fragments, or infected tissue. This procedure is often performed to alleviate pain and restore function in patients with conditions such as osteoarthritis, septic arthritis, or after joint injuries. It can be done arthroscopically, allowing for minimal invasiveness and quicker recovery compared to open surgery.
Symptoms: Hip pain, Swelling in the hip area, Stiffness or limited range of motion in the hip joint, Loss of function in the hip, Instability or feel of the hip joint giving way
Medical abbreviations: HIP, JD (Joint Debridement), ARTHRO (Arthroscopy), RA (Rheumatoid Arthritis), OA (Osteoarthritis)
Identifying features: Identifying features of patients needing hip joint lavage and debridement include a history of hip pain that is persistent or worsening, visible swelling or tenderness in the hip area, and limitations in mobility or activities due to hip dysfunction. Imaging studies may show joint degeneration or the presence of loose bodies within the joint.

{% for message in messages %}{{'<|im_start|>' + message['role'] + '

' }}{% endif %}

sentences:
Condition name: Decompression of the Suprascapular Nerve
Body region: Elbow
Region segments: {""}
Region sides: {Left,Right,Bilateral}
Description: Decompression of the suprascapular nerve is a surgical procedure aimed at relieving pressure on the suprascapular nerve, which can be compressed due to factors such as trauma, tumors, or repetitive overhead activities. This nerve innervates the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles, which are crucial for shoulder movements. When compressed, patients can experience pain and weakness in the shoulder, which can lead to decreased function in the upper limb.
Symptoms: Shoulder pain, Weakness in shoulder abduction and external rotation, Atrophy of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles, Referred pain to the elbow, Difficulty in overhead movements
Medical abbreviations: SSN - Suprascapular Nerve, SNR - Suprascapular Nerve Release, RTS - Return to Sports
Identifying features: Patients often present with localized shoulder pain, weakness in specific shoulder movements, and atrophy of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles upon examination.

Condition name: Arthroscopic Debridement
Body region: Shoulder
Region segments: {""}
Region sides: {Left,Right,Bilateral}
Description: Arthroscopic debridement of the shoulder is a minimally invasive surgical procedure performed to clean out damaged tissue, debris, or loose cartilage from the shoulder joint. This intervention is most commonly indicated for conditions such as shoulder bursitis, rotator cuff tears, or other degenerative shoulder diseases which result in the accumulation of debris and inflammation within the joint. The procedure is typically performed with the aid of an arthroscope, a small camera that allows the surgeon to visualize the inside of the joint on a monitor, utilizing small incisions for instrument access.
Symptoms: Shoulder pain, Limited range of motion, Swelling around the shoulder joint, Creaking or popping sounds during movement, Weakness in the shoulder, Difficulty performing daily activities involving overhead movements
Medical abbreviations: ARTHRO, ORIF (Open Reduction Internal Fixation), AC (Acromioclavicular), RCT (Rotator Cuff Tear), BMD (Bone Mineral Density), MRI (Magnetic Resonance Imaging)
Identifying features: Patients often present with a history of chronic shoulder pain and may exhibit tenderness over the anterior or lateral aspect of the shoulder. Physical examination may reveal restricted range of motion, muscle weakness, and signs of impingement. Imaging studies such as MRI may show rotator cuff tears or degeneration, while the presence of loose bodies or synovitis may be confirmed during an arthroscopic evaluation.

Condition name: Cubital Tunnel Release
Body region: Hand & Wrist
Region segments: {""}
Region sides: {Left,Right,Bilateral}
Description: Cubital tunnel release is a surgical procedure designed to alleviate pressure on the ulnar nerve at the elbow, which can become compressed or injured, leading to various symptoms in the hand and wrist. This procedure is typically performed when nonsurgical treatments have failed to improve the condition, which may arise from repetitive motions, direct trauma to the elbow, or anatomical variations. The surgery aims to relieve symptoms such as pain, tingling, and numbness, allowing for better function of the hand and wrist.
Symptoms: Pain in the inner side of the elbow, Numbness or tingling in the ring and little fingers, Weakness in grip strength, Difficulty with fine motor tasks, Sensitivity to pressure along the ulnar nerve pathway
Medical abbreviations: Ulnar Nerve (UN), Cubital Tunnel Syndrome (CTS), Electromyography (EMG), Nerve Conduction Study (NCS), Neurolysis (NL)
Identifying features: Key identifying features include the presence of elbow pain localized to the inner side, sensory changes in the ulnar nerve distribution (especially in the ring and little fingers), and weakness in hand grip or dexterity. A physical examination may reveal a positive Tinel's sign at the elbow.

widget.source_sentence:
2 weeks post-op from a C6-C7 microdiscectomy. Patient reports lingering NECK pain, primarily on the RIGHT side, which has been described as a dull ache, intensifying with prolonged sitting or looking down. Pain radiates down the RIGHT arm toward the elbow, with occasional tingling in the thumb and index finger, but no weakness noted.

Current mobility: Patient is experiencing moderate stiffness in the neck with reduced ROM, especially in flexion and rotation towards the RIGHT. Reports discomfort when turning head to check blind spots while driving and during sleep, often waking up when attempting to roll over.

Reported activities: Has been compliant with prescribed post-op restrictions but attempted light walking (up to 15-20 min daily). No reported issues with shoulder abduction or elevation, but hesitant due to past pain. 

Pain management: Utilizes ice and over-the-counter NSAIDs (Ibuprofen 400 mg TID) for pain control, with mild relief. 

Patient education: Informed on importance of posture and ergonomics, especially during prolonged sitting. Discussed positioning strategies during sleep to minimize discomfort. Encouraged to begin gentle cervical exercises as tolerated.

SHX:
Age: 45
Occupation: Office worker, primarily desk-based tasks. 

Notable: Previous history of cervical radiculopathy for 6 months prior to surgery, managed conservatively with PT and chiropractic. No current psychological distress or neuritic symptoms apart from prior mentioned. 

Physical exam: Cervical spine palpation reveals tenderness at C6-C7. Positive Spurling’s test on the RIGHT side. No signs of myelopathy.

NEXT appointment in 1 week for reassessment of symptoms and progression to strengthening exercises.
sentences:
Condition name: Cervical Microdiscectomy
Body region: Cervical Spine
Region segments: {C1,C2,C3,C4,C5,C6,C7,T1}
Region sides: {Left,Right,Bilateral,Central,N/A}
Description: Cervical microdiscectomy is a minimally invasive surgical procedure used to remove a herniated disc in the cervical spine (neck) that is pressing on spinal nerves or the spinal cord. This procedure is typically performed when conservative treatments such as physical therapy, medications, or injections fail to relieve symptoms of cervical radiculopathy, such as pain, numbness, or weakness in the arms. The aim of this surgery is to alleviate pressure on the affected nerve roots, improve mobility, and reduce pain.
Symptoms: Neck pain, Arm pain (radiculopathy), Numbness or tingling in the arms or fingers, Weakness in the arms or hands, Loss of reflexes in the arms, Difficulty with coordination or balance (in severe cases)
Medical abbreviations: C5-C6: Cervical vertebrae levels 5 and 6, MRI: Magnetic Resonance Imaging, CT: Computed Tomography, NCS: Nerve Conduction Study, EMG: Electromyography
Identifying features: Common identifying features of cervical microdiscectomy include the presence of confirmed cervical disc herniation via imaging studies, the patient's report of arm pain or neurologic deficits, and unsuccessful conservative treatment options.

Condition name: Neuroma Excision (Morton's neuroma)
Body region: Foot & Ankle
Region segments: {""}
Region sides: {Left,Right,Bilateral}
Description: Morton's neuroma is a painful condition affecting the ball of the foot, typically between the third and fourth toes. It occurs when the tissue around a nerve leading to the toes thickens, which can be caused by repetitive irritation, injury, or pressure from shoes that are too tight. Neuroma excision is a surgical procedure to remove the thickened nerve to relieve pain and discomfort.
Symptoms: Sharp, burning pain in the ball of the foot, Numbness or tingling in the toes, Sensation of standing on a pebble, Pain that worsens with activity or tight footwear, Relief of symptoms when removing shoes or massaging the foot
Medical abbreviations: NM, MCN (Morton's Compression Neuritis), ICD-10: G57.6 (Morton's neuropathy)
Identifying features: Pain localized between the third and fourth toes, typically exacerbated by wearing tight shoes or during activity.

Condition name: Cervical Foraminoplasty
Body region: Cervical Spine
Region segments: {C1,C2,C3,C4,C5,C6,C7,T1}
Region sides: {Left,Right,Bilateral,Central,N/A}
Description: Cervical foraminoplasty is a surgical procedure designed to relieve pressure on spinal nerves that can result from conditions such as herniated discs, bone spurs, or other forms of spinal stenosis in the cervical spine. The procedure involves widening the intervertebral foramen, which is the passageway through which spinal nerves exit the spinal column. By enlarging this canal, it alleviates nerve root compression, helping to reduce pain, weakness, and other neurological symptoms. This procedure often follows conservative treatment methods like physiotherapy or medication when they fail to provide relief.
Symptoms: Neck pain, Shoulder pain, Radiating pain down the arms, Numbness or tingling in the arms or hands, Muscle weakness in the arm or hand, Reduced range of motion in the neck
Medical abbreviations: C1, C2, C3, C4, C5, C6, C7, T1, MRI (Magnetic Resonance Imaging), CT (Computed Tomography), PLIF (Posterior Lumbar Interbody Fusion), ACDF (Anterior Cervical Discectomy and Fusion)
Identifying features: Identifying features of cervical foraminoplasty include a history of cervical radiculopathy, diagnostic imaging revealing foraminal narrowing or nerve root compression, and symptomatic relief demonstrated post-surgery through reduced pain and improved arm function.

widget.source_sentence:
Patient underwent open-heart surgery 6 weeks ago, experiencing ongoing THORACIC pain post-sternotomy. Pain localized to the sternal area, described as a constant and aching sensation, intensifying with deep breathing, coughing, or changing positions. Patient reports a feeling of tightness across the chest and difficulty with upper limb movements, especially shoulder flexion and reach overhead. 

Pain scale: 6/10 at rest, 8/10 with movement. No radiating pain reported to arms or back. 

Sleep disturbances noted, particularly when trying to lie supine or on the side. Patient prefers to prop up with pillows for support during sleep. 

Meds: Taking pain medication as prescribed post-operatively, with good adherence. 

Physical activity limited; patient has begun gentle walking for 10-15 minutes daily, which is tolerable but followed by soreness. 

No signs of infection at the sternal closure site, which appears clean and dry. 

Patient expresses concern about returning to normal daily activities and the fear of re-injury.

Age: 65 
Prior health: Previously active, enjoyed swimming and cycling, but limited upper body strength prior to surgery. 

Plan: Initiate gradual range of motion exercises for shoulders, focus on diaphragmatic breathing techniques to manage pain, monitor healing, and assess for the need for further orthopedic evaluation if symptoms persist. Follow-up in 2 weeks.
sentences:
Condition name: Sternal Reconstruction
Body region: Thoracic
Region segments: {C7,T1,T2,T3,T4,T5,T6,T7,T8,T9,T10,T11,T12,L1}
Region sides: {Left,Right,Bilateral,Central,N/A}
Description: Sternal reconstruction is a surgical procedure performed to repair or replace the sternum (breastbone) typically due to trauma, infection, or after surgical resection in cases such as cancer. The sternum serves as a protective structure for vital thoracic organs and is integral to the thoracic skeleton. The reconstruction process can involve the use of synthetic materials, bone grafts, or titanium plates to restore structural integrity and stability to the chest area.
Symptoms: Chest pain, Difficulty breathing, Sternal deformity or instability, Increased risk of respiratory infections, Scar tissue formation during healing
Medical abbreviations: CABG (Coronary Artery Bypass Grafting), CT (Computed Tomography) scan, MRI (Magnetic Resonance Imaging), Sternal Closure
Identifying features: Patients may show signs of sternal instability, abnormal chest wall contour, or have visible surgical scars. Diagnostic imaging such as X-rays or CT scans may be utilized to assess the sternum's integrity post-surgery.

Condition name: Sternal closure following open-heart surgery or sternotomy
Body region: Thoracic
Region segments: {C7,T1,T2,T3,T4,T5,T6,T7,T8,T9,T10,T11,T12,L1}
Region sides: {Left,Right,Bilateral,Central,N/A}
Description: Sternal closure following open-heart surgery or sternotomy involves the surgical technique used to close the sternum after it has been surgically split to gain access to the heart. Sternotomy is a common procedure in various cardiac surgeries, and the closure is done using steel wires, sutures, or bone plates. Proper closure is crucial for structural integrity during recovery and minimizes complications such as sternum instability or infection.
Symptoms: Chest pain or discomfort, Limited range of motion in the upper body, Swelling around the incision site, Difficulty in deep breathing, Post-surgical fatigue, Palpable hardware near the sternum (in some cases)
Medical abbreviations: CABG (Coronary Artery Bypass Grafting), MVR (Mitral Valve Replacement), AVR (Aortic Valve Replacement), CPB (Cardiopulmonary Bypass), ICU (Intensive Care Unit), PT (Physical Therapy), OT (Occupational Therapy)
Identifying features: A recent history of open-heart surgery, visible scar along the midline of the chest, tenderness or swelling around the incision area, presence of sternal wires or sutures if visible during examination.

Condition name: Latarjet Procedure (Coracoid Transfer)
Body region: Shoulder
Region segments: {""}
Region sides: {Left,Right,Bilateral}
Description: The Latarjet procedure, also known as coracoid transfer, is a surgical technique performed to stabilize the shoulder joint, primarily indicated for individuals with recurrent shoulder dislocations or significant glenoid bone loss. The procedure involves transferring a piece of the coracoid process, along with the attached musculotendinous tissue (typically the short head of the biceps), to the anterior aspect of the glenoid. This serves to increase the stability of the shoulder by augmenting the bony support as well as enhancing soft tissue restraints.
Symptoms: Recurrent shoulder dislocations or instability, Pain in the shoulder joint, Loss of range of motion, Difficulty with overhead activities, Weakness in the shoulder, Swelling or tenderness around the shoulder area
Medical abbreviations: RSD (Recurrent Shoulder Dislocation), GH (Glenohumeral), ROM (Range of Motion), AC (Acromioclavicular), RC (Rotator Cuff)
Identifying features: The Latarjet procedure is characterized by the transfer of the coracoid process to the anterior glenoid, which is often identified through imaging studies such as MRI or CT. Clinically, patients may present with a specific history of anterior shoulder instability or repeated dislocations, requiring surgical intervention.

widget.source_sentence:
Patient presents with LEFT hand pain following a fall while playing basketball 10 days ago. Impact directly on the LEFT hand during an attempt to break the fall. Immediate swelling and bruising noted over the 2nd and 3rd metacarpals. 

Reported difficulty with grip strength and hand function, particularly with activities of daily living (ADLs) such as opening jars and typing on a keyboard. Unable to make a full fist and experiences pain (VAS 7/10) with any movement involving finger flexion and extension.

Body chart:
- Localized tenderness over the 2nd and 3rd metacarpal regions
- Swelling present - moderate; no visible deformity noted
- No neural symptoms; sensation intact across the hand
- AROM: Restricted in MCP flexion (30°-40°), painful and limited in extension

SHX:
Age: 25
Occupation: Graphic designer - predominantly desk work
Hobbies: Basketball, painting 

Patient was advised on RICE (Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation) protocol initially. Incorporation of splint for support was initiated post-initial A&E visit. Currently scheduled for ORIF to stabilize the fracture. 

Concerns regarding recovery timeframe and return to sport expressed by the patient. Follow-up consultations planned post-surgery to assess for rehabilitation progress.

No previous history of fractures or orthopedic injuries.
sentences:
Condition name: Subtalar Fusion
Body region: Foot & Ankle
Region segments: {""}
Region sides: {Left,Right,Bilateral}
Description: Subtalar fusion is a surgical procedure that involves the fusion of the subtalar joint, which is located below the ankle joint. This joint plays a critical role in foot motion and stability, allowing for inversion and eversion of the foot. The fusion is usually performed to alleviate chronic pain due to conditions such as subtalar joint arthritis, post-traumatic arthritis, or other degenerative conditions. By fusing the joint, the motion at that specific site is eliminated, which can help reduce pain and improve function in other parts of the foot and ankle.
Symptoms: Chronic pain in the heel or ankle, Swelling around the subtalar joint, Stiffness in the foot, Limited range of motion in the ankle, Discomfort during activities such as walking or standing
Medical abbreviations: STJ (Subtalar Joint), ORIF (Open Reduction and Internal Fixation), NSAIDs (Non-Steroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drugs), PT (Physical Therapy), ROM (Range of Motion)
Identifying features: Patients often exhibit significant limitations in foot mobility, especially in inversion and eversion movements. There may also be tenderness, swelling, and a history of previous foot or ankle injuries or surgeries.

Condition name: Metacarpal Fracture ORIF
Body region: Hand & Wrist
Region segments: {""}
Region sides: {Left,Right,Bilateral}
Description: A metacarpal fracture ORIF (Open Reduction and Internal Fixation) is a surgical procedure to repair a fracture in one of the metacarpal bones, which are the long bones in the hand located between the wrist and the fingers. This type of injury often occurs due to trauma, such as falls, sports injuries, or punching an object. ORIF is typically performed to realign the fractured bone fragments accurately and stabilize them with plates and screws, allowing for proper healing and restoration of function in the hand.
Symptoms: Pain in the hand or wrist, Swelling and bruising, Deformity of the hand (e.g., angulated or rotated finger), Decreased range of motion, Difficulty in gripping or pinching, Numbness or tingling (if nerves are affected)
Medical abbreviations: ORIF - Open Reduction and Internal Fixation, RICE - Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation, ROM - Range of Motion, CT - Computed Tomography (if imaging is needed), X-ray - Radiographic imaging to assess the fracture
Identifying features: Common identifying features of a metacarpal fracture include localized swelling and tenderness over the affected metacarpal, a visible deformity such as a dorsal angulation of the finger, and limited finger mobility. X-rays are crucial in diagnosing the specific type and severity of the fracture.

Condition name: Hip Joint Lavage and Debridement
Body region: Hip
Region segments: {""}
Region sides: {Left,Right,Bilateral}
Description: Hip joint lavage and debridement is a surgical procedure that involves the cleaning of the hip joint by flushing it with a sterile fluid and removing any debris, such as damaged cartilage, floating bone fragments, or infected tissue. This procedure is often performed to alleviate pain and restore function in patients with conditions such as osteoarthritis, septic arthritis, or after joint injuries. It can be done arthroscopically, allowing for minimal invasiveness and quicker recovery compared to open surgery.
Symptoms: Hip pain, Swelling in the hip area, Stiffness or limited range of motion in the hip joint, Loss of function in the hip, Instability or feel of the hip joint giving way
Medical abbreviations: HIP, JD (Joint Debridement), ARTHRO (Arthroscopy), RA (Rheumatoid Arthritis), OA (Osteoarthritis)
Identifying features: Identifying features of patients needing hip joint lavage and debridement include a history of hip pain that is persistent or worsening, visible swelling or tenderness in the hip area, and limitations in mobility or activities due to hip dysfunction. Imaging studies may show joint degeneration or the presence of loose bodies within the joint.
